{"query":"Live Nation retain Ticketmaster ownership SeatGeek StubHub platform access DOJ settlement terms 2026","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1147,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.ticketnews.com/2026/06/dojs-live-nation-settlement-fine-print-leaves-ticketmaster-at-center-of-ticketing-system/","title":"DOJ’s Live Nation Settlement Fine Print Leaves Ticketmaster at Center of Ticketing System | TicketNews","content":"District Judge Arun Subramanian determines whether it serves the public interest.
The structure gives venues more flexibility, but still allows Ticketmaster to maintain long-term relationships and remain the dominant platform.
Anti-retaliation provisions prohibit Live Nation and Ticketmaster from penalizing venues for working with competing ticketing providers or promoters, though carveouts allow for independent artist decisions and ordinary business conduct."},{"url":"https://www.politico.com/news/2026/03/09/live-nation-reaches-settlement-with-doj-in-antitrust-fight-00818564","title":"Live Nation reaches settlement with DOJ in antitrust fight - POLITICO","content":"Live Nation has reached a settlement with the Department of Justice in its high-stakes antitrust case less than a week after the trial began, according to three people familiar with the matter.
Under the settlement, Ticketmaster will be required to open parts of its platform to rival ticketing companies, allowing third-party sellers such as SeatGeek or Eventbrite to list tickets directly through Ticketmaster’s technology.
The Justice Department and 40 state attorneys general first sued Live Nation under the Biden administration in May 2024, alleging the concert giant built and maintained an illegal monopoly over live events through its control of ticketing, venues and artist promotion.
Under the agreement, Live Nation will be required to divest more than 10 amphitheaters, creating more independently operated venues and loosening the company’s hold on the live music ecosystem."},{"url":"https://www.thompsoncoburn.com/insights/concert-ticket-pricing-live-nation-settles-with-doj-goes-to-trial-against-states/","title":"Concert Ticket Pricing: Live Nation Settles with DOJ, Goes to Trial Against States | Thompson Coburn LLP","content":"Department of Justice (“DOJ”) in a high-profile antitrust case in the Southern District of New York.
Under the agreement, Live Nation retains Ticketmaster but must open its venues to rival ticketing services, divest certain amphitheaters, and cap ticket fees at 15%.
However, a substantial coalition of states rejected the DOJ’s terms as insufficient and proceeded with their state law claims.
Those plaintiffs are seeking 15 years of damages arising out of the alleged overpricing of over 400 million concert and venue tickets."},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_v._Live_Nation_Entertainment","title":"United States v. Live Nation Entertainment - Wikipedia","content":"On May 23, 2024, the Department of Justice formally announced its antitrust lawsuit against Live Nation Entertainment, accusing the company of illegally monopolizing the live event market.
On March 14, 2025, Judge Arun Subramanian rejected a motion from Live Nation to dismiss the case.
On Thursday, March 5, 2026, the Department of Justice and Live Nation Entertainment reached a tentative settlement requiring Live Nation to create a $280m settlement fund for participating states.
The settlement was announced on March 9, 2026, after Judge Arun Subramanian was told on the prior evening.
Judge Arun Subramanian will determine the remedy, which the states have asked to include monetary damages and structural relief.
Commentators have noted that the lawsuit follows a number of high-profile antitrust lawsuits launched under the Biden administration, including against Apple, Amazon, and Google."}]}

Live Nation will have to pay $280 million in damages to the suing states. * Live Nation agrees to major antitrust settlement with the Justice Department that would open Ticketmaster to rival ticket sellers and force the company to open select venues to competing promoters. Live Nation has reached a settlement with the Justice Department in an antitrust case that put the entertainment giant at risk of being separated from Ticketmaster. With pending approval from the court, Live Nation will have to pay $280 million in damages to the suing states and allow competitors to sell tickets on its platform. The complaint was filed in 2024, when the federal government, 39 states including California and the District of Columbia, alleged that Live Nation and Ticketmaster have monopolies in various aspects of the live music industry, such as concert promotion, venue operations, artist management and ticketing services.","score":0.7859175,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.ticketnews.com/2026/06/dojs-live-nation-settlement-fine-print-leaves-ticketmaster-at-center-of-ticketing-system","title":"DOJ’s Live Nation Settlement Fine Print Leaves Ticketmaster at Center of Ticketing System | TicketNews","content":"# DOJ’s Live Nation Settlement Fine Print Leaves Ticketmaster at Center of Ticketing System. Home » DOJ’s Live Nation Settlement Fine Print Leaves Ticketmaster at Center of Ticketing System. The Department of Justice has released the fine print of its proposed Live Nation–Ticketmaster settlement, detailing a no-breakup agreement that critics say leaves Ticketmaster at the center of the ticketing ecosystem despite years of government allegations of monopolistic conduct. The proposed final judgment, filed Friday in federal court in Manhattan and included at the end of the article, formalizes the framework the DOJ reached earlier this year with Live Nation Entertainment and Ticketmaster. CEO Michael Rapino said at the time that the settlement marked “a major step in improving the concert experience for artists and fans throughout the United States.” He added that Live Nation’s amphitheaters would be opened to all promoters, who could decide how to distribute up to 50% of tickets, while Ticketmaster service fees at those venues would be capped at 15%.","score":0.76992935,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.instagram.com/p/DVq3J8XABO-","title":"Ticketmaster will open parts of its platform to rival ticketing companies ...","content":"On July 14, 2026, the DOJ's proposed settlement with Live Nation was formally published in the Federal Register — opening a 60-day public","score":0.72231215,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.axios.com/2026/03/09/live-nation-ticketmaster-doj-settlement","title":"How much you'd save on concerts with Live Nation, Ticketmaster settlement","content":"Live Nation and the Department of Justice have reached a settlement over the latter's antitrust claims, allow venues to partner with alternate","score":0.7135319,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/09/arts/music/live-nation-ticketmaster-antitrust-suit-settled.html","title":"Live Nation, Ticketmaster's Owner, Settles Antitrust Case With Justice ...","content":"Live Nation would also pay up to $280 million in damages to be split among the states that join the settlement, according to the people familiar","score":0.6659544,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.87,"request_id":"8ee38885-d255-4850-b7a1-1c22045afaaf"}
